news_summary: Breakthroughs in stem cell therapy show long-term remission of severe autoimmune disease, new insights reveal potential triggers of Alzheimer’s, and proteins that protect against deadly mutations have been identified.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées majeures dans le domaine de la thérapie par cellules souches permettent d'obtenir une rémission à long terme de maladies auto-immunes graves ; de nouvelles découvertes mettent en lumière les facteurs déclencheurs potentiels de la maladie d'Alzheimer ; et des protéines protégeant contre des mutations mortelles ont été identifiées.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la terapia con células madre permiten lograr una remisión a largo plazo de enfermedades autoinmunes graves; nuevos hallazgos revelan posibles factores desencadenantes del Alzheimer, y se han identificado proteínas que protegen contra mutaciones mortales.

articles.title: Stem cells banish severe autoimmune disease for 15 years
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01925-7Two people were the first to receive the therapy for a condition that damages the spinal cord and optic nerve.
articles.content:
Credit: Steve Gschmeissner/Science Photo Library

A man and a woman with a rare and devastating autoimmune disease have been in remission for more than 15 years after receiving a stem-cell transplant1. The positive results, which were reported in Med, suggest that the experimental treatment warrants a larger clinical trial, say scientists.

The two people had a severe and potentially fatal disease in which immune cells produce antibodies that trigger an attack on the spinal cord and nerve connecting the eye and the brain, leading to a condition called neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD). Symptoms tend to appear in episodes that last for days or months and include eye pain, vision loss, vomiting and weakness or paralysis affecting the arms and legs. Current treatments can prevent these episodes with ongoing medication, but they did not work in these two individuals.

After the stem-cell transplant, the man’s neurological function improved and he resumed a normal life and went on to have two children. The woman was able to use her arms more effectively than before her treatment and no longer requires medication to reduce symptoms.

“I don’t think we can say it’s a cure, but then again, it has addressed the problem the disease has caused over this very long period of time,” says Jiao Jiao Li, a biomedical engineer at the University of Technology Sydney in Australia.

As part of the treatment, called allogeneic haematopoietic stem-cell transplant, donor stem cells are collected from the blood of another person. The procedure has been used to treat some cancers, sickle-cell disease and other blood conditions. Massimo Filippi, a co-author of the study and a neurologist at the IRCCS San Raffaele Hospital in Milan, Italy, and his colleagues say this is the first use of this therapy to treat NMOSD.

The man was the first to undergo the allogeneic transplant, receiving stem cells in 2009 from his sister. The following year, the woman received cells from an unrelated donor. The two particpants received a single infusion of their donor’s stem cells.

Being able to keep these people symptom-free for a long period of time is exciting, says Bruce Milthorpe, a scientist at the University of Technology Sydney.

Immune system reset

Before the transplant, the participants received chemotherapy drugs called fludarabine and treosulfan and a monoclonal antibody drug to remove the immune system’s B cells that produce the antibodies that attack the spinal cord and optic nerve.

Before receiving their stem-cell transplants, the two individuals also received a short course of antibodies and immunosuppressant drugs to prevent the donor cells from attacking the recipient's healthy cells, also known as graft-versus-host disease — a common complication after stem-cell transplants. The complication can be life-threatening, says Li. Neither person developed antibodies associated with NMOSD, and they developed healthy immune systems, the authors of the study report.

Li says the procedure completely replaces the person’s immune system. Whereas other versions of the treatment that use a person’s own stem cells reset the immune system. However, these versions might not work as well for people with autoimmune conditions if the B cells that produce the attacking antibodies are not totally eradicated, she adds.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where two patients with a severe autoimmune disease have been symptom-free for over 15 years following an innovative stem-cell transplant therapy. This treatment shows promise for a rare, debilitating condition with currently limited options, suggesting broad potential impact if validated in larger trials. The article provides detailed context on the disease, treatment method, and long-term positive outcomes, indicating substantial real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Stem-cell transplant keeps severe autoimmune disease in remission for 15 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) is a rare disease where the immune system attacks the spinal cord and the nerve between the eye and brain. This causes symptoms like vision loss, weakness, and paralysis. Current medicines can help but did not work for two people with severe forms of this disease.

**What happened** – In 2009 and 2010, a man and a woman with NMOSD received a special treatment called an allogeneic haematopoietic stem-cell transplant. This means they got stem cells from another person’s blood to replace their damaged immune systems. Before the transplant, they had chemotherapy and drugs to remove harmful immune cells and prevent serious side effects. After the treatment, both have been free of symptoms for over 15 years. The man returned to a normal life and had children, and the woman improved her arm movement and stopped needing medication.

**Impact** – This is the first time this kind of stem-cell transplant has been used successfully for NMOSD. It shows that replacing the immune system with donor cells can stop the disease for a very long time. Unlike other treatments that try to reset the immune system using a person’s own cells, this one fully removes the faulty immune cells that cause the disease. This breakthrough offers a potential new way to treat a disease that was very hard to control.

**What's next step** – Scientists suggest testing this treatment in larger groups of patients to confirm how well it works and how safe it is. If successful, it might become a new option for people with NMOSD and possibly other similar autoimmune diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A stem-cell transplant from a donor has kept two patients with a severe autoimmune disease symptom-free for over 15 years, offering hope for new treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une greffe de cellules souches maintient une maladie auto-immune sévère en rémission depuis 15 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La neuromyélite optique spectre de la maladie (NMOSD) est une maladie rare où le système immunitaire attaque la moelle épinière et le nerf entre l'œil et le cerveau. Cela provoque des symptômes comme la perte de la vue, la faiblesse et la paralysie. Les médicaments actuels peuvent aider, mais ils n'ont pas fonctionné pour deux personnes atteintes de formes sévères.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En 2009 et 2010, un homme et une femme atteints de NMOSD ont reçu un traitement spécial appelé greffe de cellules souches hématopoïétiques allogéniques. Cela signifie qu'ils ont reçu des cellules souches du sang d'un autre donneur pour remplacer leur système immunitaire défaillant. Avant la greffe, ils ont eu une chimiothérapie et des médicaments pour éliminer les cellules immunitaires nuisibles et prévenir les effets secondaires graves. Après le traitement, ils sont restés sans symptômes pendant plus de 15 ans. L'homme a repris une vie normale et eu des enfants, et la femme a amélioré la mobilité de ses bras et a arrêté les médicaments.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois que ce type de greffe de cellules souches est utilisé avec succès pour la NMOSD. Cela montre que remplacer le système immunitaire par des cellules de donneur peut arrêter la maladie sur une très longue période. Contrairement à d'autres traitements qui tentent de réinitialiser le système immunitaire avec les propres cellules du patient, celui-ci élimine complètement les cellules fautives qui causent la maladie. Cette avancée offre une nouvelle possibilité pour traiter une maladie difficile à contrôler.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques suggèrent de tester ce traitement sur un plus grand nombre de patients pour confirmer son efficacité et sa sécurité. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait devenir une nouvelle option pour les personnes atteintes de NMOSD et peut-être d'autres maladies auto-immunes similaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une greffe de cellules souches provenant d'un donneur a maintenu deux patients atteints d'une maladie auto-immune sévère sans symptômes pendant plus de 15 ans, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Un trasplante de células madre mantiene en remisión una enfermedad autoinmune severa durante 15 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El trastorno del espectro de neuromielitis óptica (NMOSD) es una enfermedad rara donde el sistema inmunológico ataca la médula espinal y el nervio que conecta el ojo con el cerebro. Esto causa síntomas como pérdida de visión, debilidad y parálisis. Los medicamentos actuales pueden ayudar, pero no funcionaron para dos personas con formas graves.

**Qué pasó** – En 2009 y 2010, un hombre y una mujer con NMOSD recibieron un tratamiento especial llamado trasplante alogénico de células madre hematopoyéticas. Esto significa que recibieron células madre de la sangre de otra persona para reemplazar su sistema inmunológico dañado. Antes del trasplante, recibieron quimioterapia y medicamentos para eliminar las células inmunes dañinas y prevenir efectos secundarios graves. Después del tratamiento, ambos han estado sin síntomas por más de 15 años. El hombre volvió a una vida normal y tuvo hijos, y la mujer mejoró el uso de sus brazos y dejó de necesitar medicación.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que este tipo de trasplante de células madre se usa con éxito para NMOSD. Muestra que reemplazar el sistema inmunológico con células donantes puede detener la enfermedad por mucho tiempo. A diferencia de otros tratamientos que intentan reiniciar el sistema inmunológico con las propias células del paciente, este elimina completamente las células defectuosas que causan la enfermedad. Este avance ofrece una nueva manera de tratar una enfermedad que era muy difícil de controlar.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos sugieren probar este tratamiento en grupos más grandes de pacientes para confirmar su eficacia y seguridad. Si tiene éxito, podría ser una nueva opción para personas con NMOSD y posiblemente otras enfermedades autoinmunes similares.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un trasplante de células madre de un donante ha mantenido a dos pacientes con una enfermedad autoinmune severa sin síntomas por más de 15 años, ofreciendo una nueva esperanza para tratamientos futuros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two glowing, intertwined neural pathways shaped like delicate branches connecting a stylized spinal cord and an eye, surrounded by softly pulsing stem cells gently flowing between them, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones on a simple, light background.

articles.title: Scientists May Have Found What Really Triggers Alzheimer’s Disease
articles.summary: Scientists may have uncovered a hidden trigger behind Alzheimer’s disease. Instead of plaques being the root cause, amyloid beta appears to interfere with tau, a protein that helps keep neurons functioning properly. This disruption could set off the damage that eventually leads to the disease’s most recognizable brain changes.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of California, Riverside have proposed a new explanation for how Alzheimer's disease may begin. Instead of being driven primarily by plaque buildup in the brain, the disease could start when one protein interferes with the normal function of another inside nerve cells.

For years, Alzheimer's research has largely centered on amyloid beta (a-beta), a protein that forms clumps in the brains of people with the disease. The idea gained support because inherited mutations that increase a-beta levels can cause early onset Alzheimer's.

However, despite thousands of clinical trials designed to remove a-beta, those treatments have largely failed to stop the disease or reverse its progression.

Scientists have also long known that another protein called tau accumulates in the brains of Alzheimer's patients. What has remained uncertain is exactly how tau and a-beta are connected.

"In addition to having dementia, Alzheimer's diagnosis requires both a-beta and tau buildup in the brain," said UCR chemistry professor and study lead author Ryan Julian. "But many labs focus on the role of one and ignore the other."

Published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, Nexus, the new study points to a direct interaction between these two proteins.

How Amyloid Beta and Tau May Interact

Tau normally helps stabilize microscopic structures known as microtubules. These tiny tube-like structures act as transportation routes inside nerve cells, carrying essential materials to different parts of the neuron. Without functioning microtubules, neurons struggle to transport the molecules they need to survive and communicate.

The research team noticed that the section of tau responsible for attaching to microtubules closely resembles a-beta in both size and structure. That observation led them to wonder whether a-beta could also bind to microtubules.

To investigate, the scientists attached a fluorescent marker to a-beta. By tracking changes in its movement and light emission, they were able to determine when the protein attached itself to microtubules.

Their experiments revealed that a-beta and tau bind to microtubules with similar strength. As a result, when a-beta accumulates inside neurons, it can potentially push tau out of its normal position.

"Our work shows amyloid beta and tau compete for the same binding sites on microtubules, and that a-beta can prevent tau from functioning correctly," Julian said.

A New Possible Trigger for Alzheimer's

According to the researchers, Alzheimer's may begin when a-beta displaces tau from microtubules. Once that happens, the cell's internal transport network may start to break down.

At the same time, tau may begin behaving abnormally. Without its normal interaction with microtubules, the protein can clump together and move into regions of neurons where it does not normally belong.

This model suggests that the buildup of a-beta and tau may be a consequence of deeper cellular problems rather than the original cause of the disease. The idea could help explain several long-standing puzzles in Alzheimer's research.

For example, plaques made of a-beta often form outside cells. If the key damage occurs when a-beta interferes with tau inside neurons, those external plaques may not directly disrupt tau or the microtubules it supports.

Aging, Autophagy, and Microtubules

The proposed mechanism also fits with evidence that the brain's natural recycling process becomes less efficient with age.

A process known as autophagy normally removes unwanted proteins, including a-beta, from cells. As autophagy slows in older adults, a-beta may accumulate inside neurons and increasingly compete with tau for access to microtubules.

Additional observations support the theory as well. Some recent studies have reported that lithium may reduce the risk of Alzheimer's disease, while earlier research found that lithium helps stabilize microtubules.

Those findings raise the possibility that protecting microtubules could help counter some of the harmful effects caused by a-beta.

Implications for Future Treatments

If future studies confirm these results, they could influence the direction of Alzheimer's drug development.

Rather than focusing exclusively on removing protein clumps, researchers might target the interaction between a-beta and microtubules. Another potential strategy would be boosting the cell's ability to clear a-beta before it accumulates inside neurons.

Julian believes the findings help tie together many previously disconnected observations from Alzheimer's research.

"This idea helps make sense of many results that previously seemed unrelated," Julian said. "It gives us a clearer picture of what may be going wrong inside neurons and where new treatments might start."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding the underlying mechanism of Alzheimer's disease, which has broad implications for future treatment development. The discovery offers a new perspective that could lead to more effective therapies, benefiting a large population affected by this disease. The article provides detailed context about the research, its findings, and potential impact, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New discovery reveals how Alzheimer’s disease may begin inside brain cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer’s disease is a brain illness that causes memory loss and confusion. Scientists have long thought that clumps of a protein called amyloid beta (a-beta) were the main cause. But treatments trying to remove these clumps have not worked well.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of California, Riverside found that a-beta might cause trouble by pushing out another protein called tau inside nerve cells. Tau normally helps tiny tubes called microtubules carry important materials inside neurons. When a-beta takes tau’s place, the microtubules stop working properly. This can lead to damage inside the brain cells.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we understand Alzheimer’s. Instead of just focusing on removing amyloid beta clumps outside cells, scientists can now study how a-beta and tau interact inside the cells. This explains why past treatments failed and opens new ways to stop the disease earlier by protecting the cell’s transport system.

**What's next step** – Future research will test if drugs can stop a-beta from pushing tau away or help cells clear a-beta better. Some studies suggest lithium might help stabilize microtubules, so it could be tested more. These new ideas could lead to better treatments that protect the brain’s cells from the start of the disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Alzheimer’s may begin when amyloid beta disrupts tau inside brain cells, offering a new target for treatments that protect neuron function.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle découverte révèle comment la maladie d’Alzheimer commence dans les cellules du cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d’Alzheimer est une maladie du cerveau qui cause des pertes de mémoire et de la confusion. Les scientifiques pensaient que des amas d’une protéine appelée amyloïde bêta (a-bêta) étaient la cause principale. Mais les traitements visant à enlever ces amas n’ont pas bien fonctionné.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à Riverside ont découvert que l’a-bêta cause des problèmes en repoussant une autre protéine nommée tau à l’intérieur des cellules nerveuses. Tau aide normalement de petits tubes appelés microtubules à transporter des matériaux importants dans les neurones. Quand l’a-bêta prend la place de tau, les microtubules ne fonctionnent plus bien, ce qui peut endommager les cellules du cerveau.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de la maladie d’Alzheimer. Au lieu de se concentrer uniquement sur l’élimination des amas d’amyloïde bêta à l’extérieur des cellules, les scientifiques peuvent maintenant étudier comment l’a-bêta et tau interagissent à l’intérieur des cellules. Cela explique pourquoi les traitements précédents ont échoué et ouvre de nouvelles voies pour arrêter la maladie plus tôt en protégeant le système de transport cellulaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les recherches futures testeront si des médicaments peuvent empêcher l’a-bêta de repousser tau ou aider les cellules à mieux éliminer l’a-bêta. Certaines études suggèrent que le lithium pourrait stabiliser les microtubules, donc il pourrait être davantage testé. Ces nouvelles idées pourraient mener à de meilleurs traitements protégeant les cellules du cerveau dès le début de la maladie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La maladie d’Alzheimer pourrait commencer lorsque l’amyloïde bêta perturbe tau à l’intérieur des cellules du cerveau, offrant une nouvelle cible pour des traitements protégeant la fonction des neurones.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo descubrimiento muestra cómo comienza la enfermedad de Alzheimer dentro de las células cerebrales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer es un trastorno cerebral que causa pérdida de memoria y confusión. Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos pensaron que los grupos de una proteína llamada beta amiloide (a-beta) eran la causa principal. Pero los tratamientos para eliminar estos grupos no han funcionado bien.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en Riverside encontraron que la a-beta puede causar problemas al desplazar a otra proteína llamada tau dentro de las células nerviosas. Tau ayuda a tubos diminutos llamados microtúbulos a transportar materiales importantes dentro de las neuronas. Cuando la a-beta toma el lugar de tau, los microtúbulos dejan de funcionar bien y las células del cerebro pueden dañarse.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia la forma en que entendemos el Alzheimer. En lugar de enfocarse solo en eliminar los grupos de beta amiloide fuera de las células, los científicos ahora pueden estudiar cómo interactúan la a-beta y tau dentro de las células. Esto explica por qué los tratamientos anteriores fallaron y abre nuevas formas de detener la enfermedad antes, protegiendo el sistema de transporte celular.

**Próximo paso** – Las investigaciones futuras probarán si los medicamentos pueden evitar que la a-beta desplace a tau o ayudar a las células a eliminar mejor la a-beta. Algunos estudios sugieren que el litio podría ayudar a estabilizar los microtúbulos, por lo que podría probarse más. Estas nuevas ideas podrían llevar a mejores tratamientos que protejan las células cerebrales desde el inicio de la enfermedad.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El Alzheimer podría comenzar cuando la beta amiloide interfiere con tau dentro de las células cerebrales, ofreciendo un nuevo objetivo para tratamientos que protejan la función neuronal.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of intertwined glowing strands representing proteins, with one strand gently displacing another from a delicate, golden tubular lattice symbolizing microtubules inside a softly illuminated neuron-like structure, evoking a harmonious balance subtly shifting yet full of hope.

articles.title: Daily briefing: The proteins that protect us from deadly mutations
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 18 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01960-4Proteins that ‘buffer’ the effects of mutations could help to treat diseases such as cancers. Plus, goats can follow human voices and the battle over a key ocean observatory project in the United States.
articles.content:
Proteins that ‘buffer’ the effects of mutations could help to treat diseases such as cancers. Plus, goats can follow human voices and the battle over a key ocean observatory project in the United States.

Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

During cyclone Ditwah, Sri Lanka received 10% of its yearly rainfall in a single day. (Majority World/Getty)

Sea levels and ocean temperatures reached alarming highs in Asia last year, while Japan, China and Korea all recorded their hottest summer on record. Deadly floods hit Pakistan, extreme rain fell in Sri Lanka and 14 tropical cyclones impacted the Macao region. “The report clearly shows that the Asian region is one of the most vulnerable to climate change,” says oceanographer Matthew England. Early warning and coordinated action will be essential to save lives, says the report.

Nature | 6 min read

Reference: State of the Climate in Asia report

Should nicotine be regulated like drugs?

A call by the Pacific island nation of Palau for nicotine to be regulated like narcotics by the United Nations will trigger an assessment and a vote by member states. If nicotine were to be added to the UN Convention on Psychotropic Substances, it would effectively make it illegal to sell nicotine products that aren’t considered medicinal, says nicotine-treatment specialist Renee Bittoun. But tobacco-company lobbying makes it unlikely that nicotine will be added to the list, says Bittoun.

Nature | 4 min read

Domestic goats (Capra hircus) can follow the voice of a person to find a reward. A group of goats were each presented with two buckets, one empty and one containing food. When a hidden researcher spoke enthusiastically in the direction of the full bucket, the goats moved toward it 60% of the time — more often than would be expected by chance. Voice-following abilities have previously been found in dogs, but not chimpanzees, which could suggest that the skills arose through domestication.

The Guardian | 3 min read

Reference: Royal Society Open Science paper

Last month, the National Science Foundation (NSF) abruptly decided to dismantle much of the Ocean Observatories Initiative (OOI) — a network of underwater and floating infrastructure, and a fixed subsea observatory in the Pacific Northwest. Marine scientists have long debated the correct balance between spending on costly infrastructure like these, and funding research grants. But the sudden break-up of OOI has triggered widespread condemnation. The NSF has not shared its reasoning for the decision, so researchers are left wondering to what extent the move reflects scientific priorities, policy changes or the predilections of the Trump administration.

The first removals of instrumented moorings were scheduled to begin today. There has been a bipartisan effort by some lawmakers to stop the process, and Democrats from the US Senate have called the move “expensive, destructive, and — crucially — illegal”.

Science | 9 min read & Associated Press | 6 min read

Where academic freedom is up (and down)

Bangladesh, Uzbekistan and Montenegro are among the nine countries that have seen significant improvements in academic freedom in the past ten years, says a new report that looks at individual and institutional metrics. Meanwhile, 50 countries — including big hitters such as the United Kingdom — experienced declines. The report specifically calls out the “fast and steep deterioration” of academic freedom in the United States because of “state and federal attacks [that] have generated severe pressure on American universities”.

Scientific American | 6 min read

Reference: Academic Freedom Index report

Features & opinion

The proteins that protect us from mutations

Our genomes are full of mutations that have the potential to cause harm. But often they don’t, thanks to a family of proteins that ‘buffer’ the mutation’s ill effects. These proteins, called HSPs, have been on scientists’ radar for decades, but advances in techniques such as cell screening and genetic editing are giving new insight into the proteins’ roles. Researchers are now trying to unpick how buffer proteins have influenced human evolution, and are investigating how to exploit them in treatments for diseases such as breast cancer.

Nature | 12 min read

Galápagos sharks (Carcharhinus galapagensis) have been caught on camera scraping up against manta rays (Mobula birostris) to scratch themselves. The backs and bellies of mantas are covered with rough dermal denticles that might help the sharks scrape parasites off their snouts and gills. The mantas don’t seem to mind when young sharks do it, but get a bit skittish when bigger sharks get too close for comfort.

Scientific American | 5 min read & 30 sec video

Reference: Marine Biodiversity paper & Environmental Biology of Fishes paper
articles.reasoning: The article highlights scientific advances in understanding proteins that buffer harmful mutations, which could lead to new treatments for diseases like cancer. This represents a significant breakthrough in health science with broad potential impact. The article provides sufficient context on the proteins' role, ongoing research, and potential applications, making it a substantive and inspiring story.
articles.personality_title: Proteins that protect us from harmful mutations could lead to new cancer treatments
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Our bodies carry many tiny changes in our genes called mutations. Some mutations can cause diseases like cancer, but special proteins help protect us by reducing the damage these mutations might cause.

**What happened** – Scientists have been studying these protective proteins, known as heat shock proteins (HSPs), for many years. New technology like genetic editing and cell screening is helping researchers better understand how these proteins work. They are now exploring how these proteins helped humans evolve and how they can be used to develop new treatments for diseases such as breast cancer.

**Impact** – This research is important because it could lead to new ways to treat diseases caused by harmful mutations. Instead of trying to fix the mutations directly, scientists might be able to use these proteins to stop the damage mutations cause. This approach could improve treatments for many people with cancer and other mutation-related illnesses.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to study how these buffering proteins work in the body and test new treatments based on them. The goal is to create medicines that help protect cells from harmful mutations, potentially improving cancer therapies in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are uncovering how special proteins protect us from harmful gene changes, opening new paths to treat diseases like cancer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des protéines qui nous protègent des mutations dangereuses pourraient mener à de nouveaux traitements contre le cancer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Nos corps portent de nombreux petits changements dans nos gènes appelés mutations. Certaines mutations peuvent causer des maladies comme le cancer, mais des protéines spéciales aident à nous protéger en réduisant les dégâts que ces mutations pourraient causer.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les scientifiques étudient ces protéines protectrices, appelées protéines de choc thermique (HSP), depuis plusieurs années. De nouvelles technologies comme l’édition génétique et le criblage cellulaire aident les chercheurs à mieux comprendre comment ces protéines fonctionnent. Ils explorent maintenant comment ces protéines ont aidé l'évolution humaine et comment elles peuvent être utilisées pour développer de nouveaux traitements contre des maladies comme le cancer du sein.

**Impact** – Cette recherche est importante car elle pourrait conduire à de nouvelles façons de traiter les maladies causées par des mutations dangereuses. Au lieu d’essayer de corriger directement les mutations, les scientifiques pourraient utiliser ces protéines pour empêcher les dégâts causés. Cette méthode pourrait améliorer les traitements pour beaucoup de personnes atteintes de cancer et d’autres maladies liées aux mutations.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier comment ces protéines protectrices fonctionnent dans le corps et tester de nouveaux traitements basés sur elles. Le but est de créer des médicaments qui aident à protéger les cellules des mutations dangereuses, ce qui pourrait améliorer les thérapies contre le cancer à l’avenir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques découvrent comment des protéines spéciales nous protègent des changements nuisibles dans les gènes, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements contre des maladies comme le cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: Proteínas que nos protegen de mutaciones dañinas podrían conducir a nuevos tratamientos contra el cáncer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Nuestros cuerpos tienen muchos pequeños cambios en nuestros genes llamados mutaciones. Algunas mutaciones pueden causar enfermedades como el cáncer, pero unas proteínas especiales nos protegen al reducir el daño que estas mutaciones podrían causar.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos han estado estudiando estas proteínas protectoras, llamadas proteínas de choque térmico (HSP), durante muchos años. Nuevas tecnologías como la edición genética y el estudio de células ayudan a los investigadores a entender mejor cómo funcionan estas proteínas. Ahora están explorando cómo estas proteínas ayudaron a la evolución humana y cómo pueden usarse para crear nuevos tratamientos para enfermedades como el cáncer de mama.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación es importante porque podría llevar a nuevas formas de tratar enfermedades causadas por mutaciones dañinas. En lugar de intentar corregir las mutaciones directamente, los científicos podrían usar estas proteínas para detener el daño que causan. Esto podría mejorar los tratamientos para muchas personas con cáncer y otras enfermedades relacionadas con mutaciones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cómo funcionan estas proteínas protectoras en el cuerpo y probarán nuevos tratamientos basados en ellas. El objetivo es crear medicamentos que ayuden a proteger las células de mutaciones dañinas, mejorando las terapias contra el cáncer en el futuro.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Los científicos están descubriendo cómo unas proteínas especiales nos protegen de cambios dañinos en los genes, abriendo nuevas vías para tratar enfermedades como el cáncer.
